_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Greene County, AL. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses. Greene County (formed 1819, seat at Eutaw since 1838) has good record survival — the courthouse at Eutaw survived the Civil War and has not experienced a major fire.

current_courthouse.location: Eutaw, AL — Main Street. The 1869 courthouse is one of the oldest active courthouses in Alabama.

previous_courthouses.location: Erie, then other locations before Eutaw was established as seat
previous_courthouses.fate: Early records transferred to Eutaw. Some loss of loose papers during moves.
Replaced by the current 1869 building. Records transferred.

record_losses.cause: None — no major fire or significant war damage
record_losses.description: Greene County's courthouse has survived intact since the 1869 building was constructed. Earlier records from the county's 1819 formation were transferred and are largely extant. Note: Hale County was carved from Greene County in 1867, so pre-1867 Greene County records cover what is now Hale County as well.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Greene County's records survive from 1819. For ancestors who lived in what is now Hale County before 1867, search Greene County records — the jurisdictional boundary moved, but the records stayed at Eutaw.
record_losses.source: Alabama Department of Archives and History. Guide to Alabama County Records.

deeds.gaps: No significant gaps. Pre-1867 deeds cover both Greene and Hale counties.
deeds.repository: Greene County Probate Office; ADAH microfilm

wills_estates.completeness: Good — estate inventories from the 1820s are essential for documenting the county's massive enslaved population

marriages.completeness: Good for white marriages. Freedmen's marriages from 1865.
marriages.repository: Greene County Probate Office; ADAH; Freedmen's Bureau at NARA

vital_records.repository: Alabama Center for Health Statistics; ADAH

substitute_sources.description: Bureau records from the Eutaw sub-district document formerly enslaved families in Greene County.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for the transition from slavery. Greene County's massive enslaved population means these records are extensive and essential.
substitute_sources.source: NARA, Records of the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands, RG 105.

substitute_sources.description: Federal investigations of Klan violence in Greene County during Reconstruction produced detailed witness testimony and documentation of the community.
substitute_sources.repository: NARA; published in Congressional documents (Ku Klux Klan hearings, 1871–72)
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Testimony names individuals — both victims and perpetrators — providing documentation of families during Reconstruction that county records do not capture.
substitute_sources.source: U.S. Congress, Joint Select Committee to Inquire into the Condition of Affairs in the Late Insurrectionary States, 1871–1872.

substitute_sources.description: ADAH holds county record microfilm, Confederate service records, and Reconstruction-era state records.

narrative_hooks: Greene County's courthouse at Eutaw has stood since 1869 — one of the oldest active courthouses in Alabama — and the records inside it reach back to 1819. {ancestor_name}'s paper trail is likely intact.
If {ancestor_name} lived in what is now Hale County before 1867, search Greene County records at Eutaw — the boundary moved but the records stayed.
The Ku Klux Klan hearings of 1871–72 produced testimony that names Greene County families — victims of racial violence documented in federal records when local records preferred silence.

Greene County's estate inventories from the 1820s–1860s list enslaved people by name and age — {ancestor_name}'s enslaved family members may appear in these documents as property, the only record of their existence before emancipation.
source_references: Alabama Department of Archives and History. Guide to Alabama County Records.
National Archives and Records Administration. Records of the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands, Record Group 105.
U.S. Congress, Joint Select Committee to Inquire into the Condition of Affairs in the Late Insurrectionary States, 1871–1872.
